FT_IC1.evidence: Methods section states: "Cross-sectional study in children of 9-11 years old (n=2,952) and adolescents of 17-18 years old (n=261), from the Dutch ABCD Study and the Spanish INMA Project." The Dutch ABCD Study (Amsterdam Born Children and their Development) is explicitly excluded by FT-IC1; this is not the U.S. NIH-funded ABCD Study.

notes: Conference abstract published in Environmental Epidemiology. Cohort is explicitly the Dutch ABCD Study (Amsterdam Born Children and their Development) combined with the Spanish INMA Project — neither is the U.S. ABCD Study. FT-EC1 exclusion.

FT_IC1.evidence: Methods: "Data from a sample of 4524 children in the ABCD study were used... survey and interview data collected for 9- to 11-year-olds from the 21-site adolescent brain cognitive development study." NDA Data Archive referenced (https://doi.org/10.15154/1412097). Confirms U.S. ABCD Study.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using generalized linear mixed models on ABCD cross-sectional baseline data examining EMU categories (texting, social networking, video chatting, video games, TV, videos) in relation to anxiety and depression symptoms.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Child Psychiatry and Human Development (2019) 50:907-917. No evidence of being a duplicate or preprint of another included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Tables 3, 4, and 5 report separate numeric coefficients (β, t-values, 95% CIs) for phone/SM-specific variables — texting (β=0.081, t=3.418, p=0.001 in boys; Table 3), social networks (β=0.061, t=2.574, p=0.010 in boys), and video chatting (β=0.069, t=3.131, p=0.002 in boys) — each paired with anxiety (individual-trait outcome). Weekday social networks β=0.031, t=2.014, p=0.044 predicting anxiety (Table 4). These are phone/SM-specific variables with their own coefficients paired with mental health individual-trait outcomes.

FT_IC3.evidence: Numeric β coefficients, t-values, and 95% CIs reported throughout Tables 2-5 for EMU-anxiety and EMU-depression relationships. Generalized linear mixed models with quantitative results.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis (generalized linear mixed models) performed associating phone/SM-specific EMU categories with individual-trait outcomes (anxiety, depression symptoms). Numeric results reported throughout.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design. FT-EC7 requires longitudinal design as one of its conditions; this paper uses baseline cross-sectional ABCD data only. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Paper uses ABCD STQ data with specific categories including texting, social networking sites (Twitter, Instagram), and video chatting (FaceTime) as separate predictors of anxiety and depression. These qualify as phone/SM-specific variables under Step 1 of FT-IC2. INCLUDE.

FT_IC1.evidence: Methods: "We used baseline (cross-sectional) data (2016-18) from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study, a US representative sample of 11,875 children aged 9 to 10 years... data will be collected... across 21 sites throughout the United States." NIMH Data DOI: 10.15154/1412097 cited.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using negative binomial structural equation modeling (SEM) to examine associations between screen time types/content and problem behaviors, with sleep duration as mediator, on ABCD cross-sectional baseline data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity (2019) 16:105. No evidence of being a duplicate or preprint.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: "Social media" is defined in methods as texting + visiting social networking sites (e.g., Twitter, Instagram) + video chat (e.g., FaceTime) — exclusively phone/SM items. Table 2 reports separate IRR estimates for social media as IV: social media → rule-breaking behavior B=0.08, p<.001, IRR=1.084 (95% CI 0.046, 0.115); social media → aggressive behavior B=0.06, p<.001, IRR=1.062 (95% CI 0.029, 0.053). These phone/SM-specific estimates are paired with individual-trait behavioral outcomes (CBCL problem behaviors).

FT_IC3.evidence: Numeric IRR estimates (exponentiated B values), B coefficients, p-values, and 95% CIs for the SM-to-problem-behavior relationships reported in Tables 2 and 3.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential negative binomial SEM with quantitative IRR results for social media (phone/SM-specific composite) predicting individual-trait problem behavior outcomes. Mediation indirect effects also reported quantitatively in Table 3.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design (baseline ABCD data). FT-EC7 requires longitudinal design; this condition is not met. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Social media variable = texting + social networking sites + video chat (collapsed due to low use), all phone/SM items — qualifies under Step 1 of FT-IC2. SM is IV predicting CBCL problem behaviors. INCLUDE.
